title: The fallout of the fallout: Zelensky's reshuffle and protests in Ukraine – European Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Zelensky's dismissal of defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov after only six months triggered protests and a public dispute with commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrsky over military reform, raising governance and efficiency questions for Ukraine's defence spending (roughly 80% of its annual budget) at a moment when parliament won't confirm his replacement until mid-August at the earliest.

themes.theme: The Fedorov dismissal and the Syrsky rift

insights.claim: President Volodymyr Zelensky dismissed his popular young defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov after just six months in the job, citing a breakdown of relations between the ministry of defence and the military leadership as the key reason.
insights.speaker: Jana Kobzová
insights.so_what: A six-month tenure signals acute instability in Ukraine's defence-ministry leadership, a governance risk for partners assessing continuity of reform and procurement programs.
insights.locator: This time it is the dismissal of his popular young defence minister

insights.claim: Fedorov publicly accused commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrsky of choosing loyalty over performance, clinging to outdated warfighting methods, and sabotaging necessary reforms, framing the conflict as a fight over Ukraine's ability to fight Russia asymmetrically.
insights.speaker: Jana Kobzová
insights.so_what: An open civil-military rift over reform and warfighting doctrine is a red flag for the effectiveness of Western military assistance pipelines into Ukraine.
insights.quote: Instead of working out how to asymmetrically defeat Russia, [Syrsky] has worked out how to split the country.

insights.claim: Fedorov's allegations against Syrsky call into question whether Western military assistance for Kyiv is being used in the best possible way, implying oversight and efficiency gaps in how allied aid is deployed.
insights.speaker: Jana Kobzová
insights.so_what: Investors/donors tracking Western aid effectiveness should treat this dispute as evidence of internal friction that could be dampening the marginal impact of assistance.
insights.locator: But Fedorov's allegations also call into question

themes.theme: Succession and the stakes for defence spending

insights.claim: Zelensky proposed Yevhen Khmara, a respected military commander and acting head of Ukraine's security service, to replace Fedorov, subject to parliamentary approval.
insights.speaker: Jana Kobzová
insights.so_what: Khmara's confirmation outcome is a concrete near-term event to track for Ukraine's defence-ministry stability.
insights.locator: Zelensky has proposed Yevhen Khmara to replace Fedorov

insights.claim: Defence accounted for roughly 80% of Ukraine's annual budget by the end of the second quarter of 2026, with almost a third of this coming from external partners, making how the new defence minister manages spending consequential for the EU and European backers who are Kyiv's biggest financial and military supporters.
insights.speaker: Jana Kobzová
insights.so_what: With ~80% of Ukraine's budget going to defence and a third externally funded, European taxpayers have direct fiscal exposure to how competently the next minister manages procurement and reform.
insights.locator: at the end of the second quarter, defence accounted for roughly 80%

insights.claim: The author argues Fedorov's replacement must be a credible, competent, trusted partner proven as a commander who can continue defence reforms, address mobilisation and corruption issues, and stay committed to building Ukraine's defence-technology base.
insights.speaker: Jana Kobzová
insights.so_what: Continuity of Ukraine's defence-tech buildout (a key driver of recent battlefield gains) hinges on whoever is confirmed — a signal to watch for defence-tech sector observers.
insights.locator: This means he or she should be proven as a commander

insights.claim: Before becoming defence minister in January 2026, Fedorov led Ukraine's digital-governance revolution and is widely credited with driving Ukraine's recent advances in defence technology that helped turn the tide against Russia.
insights.speaker: Jana Kobzová
insights.so_what: Losing Fedorov risks slowing the defence-tech modernization trajectory that has been a major factor in Ukraine's battlefield performance.
insights.locator: Before he became defence minister in January 2026

insights.claim: Ukraine's parliament approved a new government led by Sergy Koretsky on July 15th 2026, but this did not include the constitutionally required nominations for defence and foreign ministers, and barring an extraordinary session MPs will not reconvene until mid-August, leaving a confirmation gap.
insights.speaker: Jana Kobzová
insights.so_what: A confirmation vacuum until at least mid-August 2026 is a concrete calendar risk for Ukraine's defence-ministry continuity.
insights.locator: While parliament approved a new government led by Sergy Koretsky

insights.claim: Rumours about Fedorov's political ambitions have circulated for weeks, and protesters see this as a motivating factor behind Zelensky's reshuffle, creating a public-legitimacy problem even though Fedorov's own statement remained loyal to Zelensky.
insights.speaker: Jana Kobzová
insights.so_what: Perceived political motives behind personnel decisions during wartime add domestic legitimacy risk that could resurface as further unrest.
insights.locator: rumours about the outgoing official's political ambitions

insights.claim: This is the second consecutive July in which a controversial Zelensky decision has sparked protests across Ukraine, following last year's attempt to weaken two anti-corruption bodies.
insights.speaker: Jana Kobzová
insights.so_what: A recurring pattern of governance controversies each July suggests structural friction between executive decision-making and public/institutional accountability in wartime Ukraine.
insights.locator: For the second July running, a controversial decision
